🔬 EDOS Repeatability Benchmark Report

Generated: 2026-04-20 14:37  |  Budget: 20 iter Init: 3 Sobol pts Runs: 5 per algorithm Seeds: [42, 137, 271, 404, 999]

Purpose of this benchmark: To assess the repeatability (run-to-run variability) of the two EDOS optimization modes on three realistic chemistry datasets from the Olympus benchmarking framework. A well-repeatable algorithm should show tight convergence curves across all 5 independent runs (small standard deviation, low coefficient of variation CV%).

Algorithms compared:
• EDOS (gradient default): Continuous GP fitted with BoTorch; next point selected by maximising qLogExpectedImprovement via gradient-based L-BFGS-B optimizer. Uses a Matérn 5/2 kernel with Input Normalization and Output Standardisation.
• EDOS (EDBO+ like): Same GP model, but the parameter space is discretised into a regular grid (8 levels per dimension, exhaustive cross-product scope). The next point is selected from the un-evaluated grid by discrete optimisation of qLogEI — directly analogous to the EDBO+ workflow.
Oracle note: Because the Olympus neural-network emulators require TensorFlow Probability (not installed), each algorithm query is answered by nearest-neighbour lookup in the real experimental dataset. This means every evaluation returns an actual laboratory measurement, making the benchmark conservative and realistic. The closest point in (normalised) Euclidean space is returned, which naturally introduces some noise into the optimization landscape.

Repeatability Statistics

Best value found (mean ± std over 5 runs). CV = coefficient of variation (lower is more repeatable). ■ CV <5%: excellent   ■ 5–15%: moderate   ■ >15%: variable

Overview — All Datasets & Algorithms

Rows: algorithm (top = gradient, bottom = EDBO+ like). Columns: dataset. Each line = one independent run. Shaded band = mean ± 1σ. Green dotted line = dataset optimum (best value in the experimental data).

SnAr Reaction (snar)

Nucleophilic aromatic substitution (SnAr) in a flow reactor.
Parameters: residence time, morpholine equivalents, concentration, temperature.
Objective: minimise the e-factor (environmental factor / impurity yield).
Dataset: 66 real experimental measurements (Schweidtmann et al., 2018).

N-Benzylation (benzylation)

N-benzylation reaction in a flow reactor.
Parameters: flow rate, benzyl bromide equivalents, solvent equivalents, temperature.
Objective: minimise the yield of the undesired impurity.
Dataset: 73 real experimental measurements (Schweidtmann et al., 2018).

Suzuki Coupling (suzuki)

Palladium-catalysed Suzuki–Miyaura cross-coupling.
Parameters: temperature, Pd loading, ArBpin equivalents, K₃PO₄ equivalents.
Objective: maximise the reaction yield.
Dataset: 247 real experimental measurements (Häse et al., 2020).

Methodology Notes

Initial design: Both algorithms start with 3 points drawn from a Sobol quasi-random sequence within the parameter space bounds, providing good initial coverage for GP fitting.

GP model: SingleTaskGP (BoTorch) with Matérn-5/2 kernel, Input Normalisation, and Output Standardisation (zero-mean, unit-variance).

Acquisition function: qLogExpectedImprovement — a numerically stable quasi-Monte Carlo EI that avoids numerical issues at very small improvement values.

Gradient mode: Uses optimize_acqf with 16 random restarts and 256 Sobol raw samples. The optimiser is L-BFGS-B operating on the continuous acquisition landscape.

EDBO+ like mode: Uses optimize_acqf_discrete over a pre-built scope grid (8 levels/dimension → up to 8⁴ = 4096 points for 4-parameter problems); already-evaluated points are excluded from consideration.

Convergence metric: Cumulative best objective value (running minimum for minimisation, running maximum for maximisation) vs. iteration number.

Repeatability metric: Coefficient of Variation (CV%) = σ/|μ| × 100 of the final best value across 5 runs.
